hints.junior: A portfolio site, a docs site, or a blog doesn't need a server. HTML + CSS + JS files on a CDN is an architecture, and it's faster, cheaper, and more reliable than spinning up a Node server.
hints.mid: The decision matrix: static (11ty, Astro, Hugo) if content doesn't depend on the request; hybrid (Next.js, Remix ISR) if some routes are dynamic; full server if auth or real-time state is required for most pages.
hints.staff: Jamstack CDN delivery has no cold start, no scaling event, no server maintenance. The operational complexity is zero. The failure mode is cache invalidation, which is a solved problem for most CDNs (instant purge via API).

hints.junior: The 'With Backend' demo shows a simple blog using an Express server: one 200ms round trip to get posts that never change. The 'Static' demo serves the same posts as pre-built HTML. Zero round trips.
hints.mid: Open the Network tab. Backend version: 200ms+ to first byte (server round trip). Static version: 15–40ms to first byte (CDN edge). Same content, same data; architecture is the difference.
hints.staff: Measure TTFB. The static version hits a CDN PoP 5–30ms from the user. The server version hits an origin region, adds route processing, and database lookup (even cached). For public content, static always wins on TTFB.

hints.junior: The concept chapter is the principle. Read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
hints.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
hints.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

hints.junior: Skim the code patterns here; you can copy the structure even if the syntax is new.
hints.mid: Compare broken vs fixed implementation: the diff is usually smaller than you expect.
hints.staff: Check edge cases: SSR, hydration, design tokens, and high-contrast mode.

anecdotes.junior: I migrated a company blog from a WordPress server to 11ty on Netlify. Monthly cost went from $40/month to $0. Page load time dropped from 3.2s to 0.8s. The only trade: I had to rebuild on content changes, which Netlify does automatically on git push.
anecdotes.mid: The pattern that catches teams: 'we need dynamic content,' but it's just a contact form and an email. Netlify Forms, Formspree, or a single serverless function handles it. No full server needed.
anecdotes.staff: The staffing/ops argument: a static site has zero deployment risk (rollback is a CDN cache invalidation), zero scaling events (CDN absorbs HN front-page traffic), and zero OS/runtime patching. For content sites, this is the right architecture until proven otherwise.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're looking at when you don't need a backend at all, and how a static site on a CDN is faster, cheaper, and more reliable than a server for most content.
sections.mid: This case covers the static vs dynamic architecture decision, when Jamstack is the right call, and what serverless functions handle when you need just a little dynamism.
sections.staff: We're covering the TTFB math, the CDN delivery model, cache invalidation patterns, and the operational cost argument for defaulting to static until you genuinely need a server.
